explanation_md: Subtraction is left-associative, so `10 - 3 - 2` is parsed as `(10 - 3) - 2 = 7 - 2 = 5`. A right-to-left reading would give `10 - (3 - 2) = 10 - 1 = 9`, but that is not how subtraction works in C++.
`*` has higher precedence than `+`, so `2 + 3 * 4` is `2 + 12 = 14`. Parentheses in `(2 + 3) * 4` override this to give `5 * 4 = 20`.

prompt_md: What does `1 < x < 10` mean in C++ when `x` is an `int`?

options.a: It checks whether x is strictly between 1 and 10.
options.b: It is a syntax error and will not compile.
options.c: It is parsed as `(1 < x) < 10`, comparing a bool (0 or 1) against 10.
options.d: It is parsed as `1 < (x < 10)`, comparing 1 against a bool.

explanation_md: Comparison operators are left-associative: `1 < x < 10` becomes `(1 < x) < 10`. The first comparison yields `0` or `1`, which is then compared to `10`. The expression almost always evaluates to `true` and never correctly tests a range. To check a range, write `x > 1 && x < 10`.

prompt_md: Which statement correctly describes the associativity of the assignment operator `=`?

options.a: Left-to-right, so `x = y = 5` sets x first then y.
options.b: Right-to-left, so `x = y = 5` sets y first then x.
options.c: Assignment has no associativity because you cannot chain it.
options.d: Right-to-left, but only when exactly two variables are on the left side.

explanation_md: Assignment associates right-to-left. `x = y = 5` is parsed as `x = (y = 5)`: first `y` receives 5, then `x` receives the result of that assignment (also 5). Both end up as 5.

options.a: `<=` has higher precedence than `if`, so the condition is never evaluated correctly.
options.b: `60 <= grade <= 100` is parsed as `(60 <= grade) <= 100`; the bool result (0 or 1) is always less than 100.
options.c: The `if` statement requires parentheses around each comparison separately.
options.d: `grade` should be declared as `double` for range comparisons to work.

explanation_md: The chained comparison `60 <= grade <= 100` is parsed left-to-right as `(60 <= grade) <= 100`. For grade = 200, `60 <= 200` is `true` (promoted to `1`), and `1 <= 100` is also `true`. The fix is `grade >= 60 && grade <= 100`.
